coredata.dc:title: Insight into bay-/end-substituted perylene diimide and its S-scheme heterojunction for enhanced photocatalytic H2O2 production under visible-light irradiation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.

coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Bo
Li, Kang
Li, Renfu
Wang, Shoufeng
Kang, Longtian
coredata.dc:description: Here, 1D bis(N-carboxymethyl) peryleneimide (H2PDI), 0D 1,6,7,12-tetrachloro-bis(N-carboxymethyl) peryleneimide (4Cl-H2PDI), and 2D 4Cl-H2PDI/graphene quantum dot (4Cl-H2PDI/GQD) nanostructures are synthesized and carefully analyzed. The effect of bay-/end-substitution and S-scheme heterojunction of PDI-based materials as main catalysts on the photocatalytic H2O2 evolution is first studied through the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). Under the visible-light irradiation (> 420 nm), 4Cl-H2PDI and 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD as photocatalysts exhibit the ∼7 and ∼16 times H2O2 evolution rate than H2PDI (1059.6 vs. 2484.0 vs. 160.0 µM g−1 h−1), respectively. The systematical experiments reveal that 4Cl-H2PDI and 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD should prefer a two-step single-electron ORR process, while H2PDI may involve a 4e‒ water oxidation and one-step 2e‒ ORR process. Further experiments confirm that the bay-substitution and GQD doping of H2PDI can promote the generation, transportation, and separation of photogenerated electrons and holes, and prolong the carrier lifetime. This work provides insight into PDI-based photocatalytic H2O2 production.

(A) XRD patterns of GQD, 4Cl-H2PDI, and 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD samples, (B) Raman spectra of 4Cl-H2PDI and 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD samples, (C, D) FT-IR spectra of H2PDI, 4Cl-H2PDI, 4Cl-PTCDA, and a series of 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD (a–e: 4Cl-H2PDI, 4Cl-H2PDI/0.05 mL GQD, 4Cl-H2PDI/0.2 mL GQD, 4Cl-H2PDI/0.5 mL GQD, and GQD).
(A) The XPS survey spectrum, (B–D) C 1s, Cl 2p, and O 1s XPS spectra of 4Cl-H2PDI and 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD composite.

(A) UV–vis spectra of 4Cl-H2PDI and H2PDI solution; (B) UV–vis spectra and (C, D) PL spectra in the aqueous phase; (E) The transient photocurrent; (F) The impedance curves of GQD, 4Cl-H2PDI and 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD.

Photocatalytic H2O2 evolution of (A) 4Cl-H2PDI, H2PDI, and 4Cl-PTCDA; (B) Different 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD composites with the test time; (C) Photocatalytic performance of 4Cl-H2PDI/0.2-mL GQD under the different experimental conditions; (D) The ability of different samples to prevent H2O2 from degradation under light irradiation; (E) Cyclic stability; (F) AQE of 4Cl-H2PDI/0.2-mL GQD composite.

(A) Schematic of the energy band (a) before and (b) after static contact, and (c) the photoinduced charge transfer in 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD heterojunction under light irradiation, (B) Energy band structure of H2PDI, 4Cl-H2PDI and GQD, (C) RDE tests of H2PDI, 4Cl-H2PDI and 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD for the detection of the electron transfer number, (D, E) EPR spectra for the detection of ·O2 ‒ and ·OH generation in the presence of H2PDI, 4Cl-H2PDI, and 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD within 3 min.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, 1D bis(N-carboxymethyl) peryleneimide (H2PDI), 0D 1,6,7,12-tetrachloro-bis(N-carboxymethyl) peryleneimide (4Cl-H2PDI), and 2D 4Cl-H2PDI/graphene quantum dot (4Cl-H2PDI/GQD) nanostructures are synthesized and carefully analyzed. The effect of bay-/end-substitution and S-scheme heterojunction of PDI-based materials as main catalysts on the photocatalytic H2O2 evolution is first studied through the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). Under the visible-light irradiation (> 420 nm), 4Cl-H2PDI and 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD as photocatalysts exhibit the ∼7 and ∼16 times H2O2 evolution rate than H2PDI (1059.6 vs. 2484.0 vs. 160.0 µM g−1 h−1), respectively. The systematical experiments reveal that 4Cl-H2PDI and 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD should prefer a two-step single-electron ORR process, while H2PDI may involve a 4e‒ water oxidation and one-step 2e‒ ORR process. Further experiments confirm that the bay-substitution and GQD doping of H2PDI can promote the generation, transportation, and separation of photogenerated electrons and holes, and prolong the carrier lifetime. This work provides insight into PDI-based photocatalytic H2O2 production.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), as a versatile and green chemical, has been widely applied in chemical synthesis, paper bleaching, wastewater treatment, clean energy, disinfectant, etc. [1–3]. In particular, its need has dramatically increased since the COVID-19 pandemic [4]. However, traditional anthraquinone synthesis has a series of disadvantages, such as flammable and explosive intermediates, high pollution, high energy consumption, and high investment. The direct synthesis of H2O2 with H2 and O2 also faces great explosion risk [1,5–7]. Therefore, it is very important and urgent for us to look for safe, green, and low-cost routes. Among them, converting oxygen and water into H2O2 with renewable energy is considered to be one of the most promising methods. At present, great progress has been made through the electrocatalytic 2e‒ oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) process on the basis of carbon and/or metal-N-C materials [8,9]. The photocatalytic H2O2 production has also attracted increasing attention as a cheap and energy-saving route. Developing efficient photocatalysts has always been the pursuit, however, it still faces great challenges due to the more complicated process and higher requirements for photocatalyst than electrocatalyst.

In the past decade, a variety of semiconductor materials, e.g. TiO2, ZnO, WO3, graphitic carbon nitride (g-CN) have been investigated as photocatalysts for the H2O2 production because of their suitable energy band structure, high stability and facile synthesis [5,10–14]. Different strategies, involving doping, modification, heterojunction constructure, etc., have been developed to promote their visible-light absorption, the separation and transportation of photoinduced carriers, and the activity of catalytic sites [11]. For example, the B-doped g-CN nanoplatelets can exhibit an excellent photocatalytic H2O2 production rate of 4240.3 µM g−1 h−1 [12]. The g-CN/CD heterojunction can improve the interfacial electron transport and yield H2O2 with a rate of 3.0 mM g−1 h−1 [13]. Besides the above outstanding inorganic and polymer semiconductors, molecule semiconductors have also attracted increasing concern in the photocatalytic field owing to their wide absorption for solar light, easy modification, and adjustable structure [14,15]. The typical porphyrin-, phthalocyanine-, and perylene-based materials, e.g. metal organic frameworks (MOFs), covalent organic frameworks (COFs), and supramolecular structures, showed high photocatalytic activity for water splitting to oxygen and/or hydrogen, self-Fenton reaction, etc. [16–18]. For photocatalytic H2O2 production, unfortunately, the molecule semiconductors were usually used as dopants or modifications for enlarging light absorption and promoting electron/hole separation rather than main catalysts [11,14,18]. For instance, Xia et al. [14] constructed the S-scheme heterojunction of ZnTCPP modified-CN, and obtained 0.5 mM H2O2 within 90 min.

Among the organic materials, a kind of excellent semiconductors is the n-type perylene-based compounds, especially perylenetetracarboxylic dianhydride (PTCDA) and/or peryleneimide (PDI) and their derivatives. In recent years, they have attracted a lot of attention in photocatalytic H2O2 synthesis owing to high electron mobility, stable structure, and strong electron-accepting ability [15,18–21]. For example, Hu et al. [19] reported that the donor-acceptor structure of 0.6 wt% PTCDA/g-CN can significantly enhance 2e− ORR photocatalytic process with the H2O2 evolution rate of 1.6 mM g−1 h−1 and the high apparent quantum yield of 27.18 % (λ = 400 nm). Silva’s group [22] also reported that the photocatalytic H2O2 rate of 14.2 mM g−1 h−1 can be obtained with 10 wt% PTCDA-functionalized g-CN composite as a catalyst. Obviously, the introduction of PTCDA can markedly promote the performance of photocatalytic H2O2 evolution. Furthermore, it is also found that the transformation of PTCDA to PDI through the side-chain substituent methods can not only improve the hydrophilicity, but also promote the generation, separation, and transportation of photoinduced carriers [23,24]. For instance, Zhang’s group [25] synthesized the PDI derivates with the asymmetric structure at the ends to improve the dipole moment, and the H2O2 yield was increased by 8.5 times. As a dopant in the S-scheme heterojunction of PDI (< 1.0 wt%)/PCN, the performance of photocatalytic H2O2 production can be further enhanced [26]. Feng et al. [27] also synthesized the different bay-substituted PDI as doped agent, and achieved the photocatalytic H2O2 evolution rate of ∼4.5 mM g−1 h−1 with Se-HPDI (0.6 wt%)/g-CN heterojunctions as a catalyst.

According to the above-mentioned works, it can be found that the construction of S-scheme heterojunctions can effectively promote photocatalytic H2O2 production, and that the doping of PDI-based materials can also play an important role in the photocatalytic activity [11,14,18,25–27]. The details can be found in Table S1 in the Supplementary Material. Unfortunately, the effect of PDI-based materials and their S-scheme heterojunctions on photocatalytic H2O2 production has been rarely reported. Graphene quantum dot (GQD) have been widely studied as the efficient photo/electrocatalyst for the 2e‒ ORR process due to their excellent electron transport ability, high stability, high hydrophilicity, and abundant modifiable sites [28]. For example, Wang’s group [29] constructed the B, N, and –OH co-doped GQD and achieved the electrocatalytic H2O2 generation rate of 709 mM g−1 h−1 in a flow cell setup. As the electron acceptor, GQD can also play a crucial role in the heterojunction photocatalysis for oxygen, hydrogen, and H2O2 evolution [28,30,31]. In this work, we synthesized H2PDI and 4Cl-H2PDI through the end substitution of PTCDA and 4Cl-PTCDA. For the first time, the effect of end and bay substitution of PDI on the photocatalytic H2O2 synthesis was investigated and analyzed. Furthermore, the S-scheme heterojunction of 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD was constructed and optimized for H2O2 production. The morphology, structure, component, energy band structure, and optical, photoelectrical, electrical properties of H2PDI, 4Cl-H2PDI, and 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD samples were systematically analyzed. 4Cl-H2PDI and 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD samples exhibited ∼7 and ∼16 times rate of H2O2 evolution, as compared with H2PDI. A series of experiments revealed the influence of bay/end substitution of PDI and 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD S-scheme heterojunctions in the photoinduced carrier and the photocatalytic mechanism of H2O2 evolution.

3,4,9,10-Perylenetetracarboxylic dianhydride (PTCDA, 98 %), Glycine (99.5 %), and Potassium hydrogen phthalate (C8H5O4K, 99.8 %) were purchased from J & K Chemical Co. 30 % H2O2, potassium iodide (KI), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), and hydrochloric acid (HCl) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. (Shanghai, China) 1,6,7,12-tetrachloroperylene tetracarboxylic acid dianhydride (4Cl-PTCDA, 96 %), p-benzoquinone (p-BQ, 99 %), 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO, 97 %), isopropanol (IPA, 99.7 %), and imidazole (99 %) were bought from Aladdin Reagent Co. Citric acid (CA, 99.5 %) was purchased from West Asia Reagents. Ultrapure water (UP) was produced with a water purification unit (WP-UP-IV-20). All of the above chemical reagents were used without further purification.

Here, the synthesis process of H2PDI can be found in Supplementary Information. Referring to the synthesis method of H2PDI with PTCDA as a raw material [32], we prepared 4Cl-H2PDI with 4Cl-PTCDA. In a typical test, 530.0 mg 4Cl-PTCDA, 5.0 g imidazole, and 325.0 mg glycine were added into a 100.0 mL round-bottom flask and reacted under Ar for 30 min. Subsequently, the system was heated to 120 °C and reacted for 10 h. After it was cooled to about 70 °C, 100.0 mL of deionized water was added. The precipitate can be clearly observed when the pH value was adjusted to 2–3 with 4.0 M HCl. Finally, 4Cl-H2PDI can be obtained through centrifugation, washing, and drying for direct use.

Here, the synthesis process of GQD can be found in Supplementary Information [33]. In a typical experiment, 100.0 mg of 4Cl-H2PDI was first dispersed in 50.0 mL of deionized water. Then, GQD in the aqueous phase (8.6 mg mL−1) were quickly injected under vigorous stirring, followed by the addition of 100.0 µL of 0.25 M NaOH aqueous solution. It can be clearly observed that the color of the 4Cl-H2PDI solution became dark red. After stirring for 24 h, 4.0 M HCl was added until the pH value was 2–3. Finally, a 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD composite can be obtained after centrifugation, washing, and drying. Different GQD-doped 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD samples can be prepared by adjusting the volume of GQD (0.05, 0.2, and 0.5 mL) and named as 4Cl-H2PDI/XX-mL GQD, respectively.

Typically, 50.0 mg of sample was first dispersed into 100.0 mL of isopropanol aqueous solution (10 vol%), and then oxygen was pumped for 30 min under the dark condition. Subsequently, the reaction vessel was irradiated by a 300 W xenon lamp (Perfect Light, PLS-SXE300BF) with a 420 nm cut-off filter at room temperature. Every 20 min, 3.0 mL of the reaction solution was taken out and filtered. The filtrate was tested for H2O2 detection through the iodimetry method [34]. In a cyclic performance test, the catalyst after the test was centrifuged and washed, and then dispersed in the isopropanol aqueous solution again. The photocatalytic activity was measured at different wavelengths of 420, 475, 500, 520, 550, and 650 nm, respectively. Then the apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) can be calculated according to the following Eq. (1)
(1)

where n is the H2O2 molar amount (µmol), N A represents the Avogadro constant (6.02 × 1023 mol−1), S means the light irradiation area (26.4 cm2), P stands for the light intensity (mW cm−2), t shows the visible light illumination reaction time (s), λ, h and c are the wavelength (nm), the Planck constant value of 6.626 × 10−34 (J s), and the light speed (3 × 108 m s−1), respectively.

The corresponding measurement was carried in 0.5 M Na2SO4 (pH = 6.8) solution with the conventional three-electrode system on the CHI760E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument, China). Here, platinum mesh and Ag/AgCl electrode were used as counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. The working electrode was prepared by the following steps. The 2.0 mg sample was first dispersed into 1.0 mL Nafion/H2O (1:9) solution to form a uniform dispersion. 20.0 µL was coated on ITO glass (1.5 cm × 1.5 cm) as work electrode after drying. A 300 W xenon lamp (λ > 420 nm) was used as a light source for the photocurrent test. In the electrochemical impedance test, 20.0 µL was dropped on a glassy carbon as work electrode.

Based on our previous work [32], H2PDI and 4Cl-H2PDI are respectively prepared with PTCDA and 4Cl-PTCDA as raw materials, as shown in Scheme 1 . The corresponding nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) result in Fig. S1 proves the successful synthesis of 4Cl-H2PDI. Meanwhile, a series of 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD composites were obtained when the GQD amount (8.6 mg mL−1) was 0.05, 0.2 and 0.5 mL, respectively. In Fig. S2, the negative Zeta potential of 4Cl-H2PDI (∼ ‒33 mV) and the positive one of GQD (∼25 mV) mean that they can easily recombine in the aqueous phase through the electrostatic attraction force. The detailed process can be found in the Experimental section and Supplementary Information. Herein, the morphology, structure, and composition of the as-synthesized samples are first analyzed with field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1(A) and Fig. S3, H2PDI molecules tend to assemble into one-dimensional (1D) nanostructures with a length of ∼450 nm and a diameter of ∼35 nm [35]. After 4Cl-bay substitution of H2PDI at 1, 6, 7, and 12 positions, SEM images in Fig. 1(B) and Fig. S4 only present ∼30 nm zero-dimensional (0D) nanoparticles (NPs), meaning that the π-π stacking of PDI molecules should be suppressed due to the formation of twisted PDI dimers [36]. Interestingly, 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD sample contains the clear two-dimensional (2D) nanostructures, as observed in Fig. 1(C) and Fig. S5. It can be attributed to the temple role of GQD, indicating the existence interaction of between GQD and 4Cl-H2PDI [30]. Obviously, the morphology of PDI aggregates can be changed through the bay substitution and GQD doping. To further verify the successful synthesis and recombination of GQD with 4Cl-H2PDI, TEM images are carefully analyzed. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image in Fig. 1(D) reveals the existence of 1–2 nm NPs with a d-spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (1100) facet of GQD [37]. During the TEM measurement of the 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD sample, it can be also found that the sample is melted and a large number of black NPs with the average diameter of 1–2 nm exists under high-energy electron diffraction (see Fig. 1(E) and Fig. S6). The corresponding element mappings of 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD in Fig. 1(F) and Fig. S7 display the uniform distribution of C, N, O, and Cl atoms. The morphology change and the existence of stable NPs and Cl atom in 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD sample strongly suggest the successful recombination of GQD and 4Cl-H2PDI.

To explore the formation mechanism of H2PDI, 4Cl-H2PDI, and 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD nanostructure, the X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns, Raman and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of different samples were measured, as illustrated in Fig. 2 and Fig. S8. The XRD patterns of 4Cl-PTCDA and H2PDI samples in Fig. S8 exhibit more and clearer peaks than those of the 4Cl-H2PDI sample, indicating the poor crystallinity of 4Cl-H2PDI NPs. The P1 peak of H2PDI at ∼27.5° corresponds to the π-π packing of perylene moiety with a typical d-spacing of (3.34–3.55 Å). As compared with the XRD pattern of H2PDI, the π-π stacking peak in 4Cl-H2PDI shifts from ∼27.5° to ∼25.5°, as shown in Fig. S8 [38,39]. It means that the steric hindrance of 4Cl-H2PDI molecules stacking should increase and the π-π interaction should become weaker owing to the 4Cl-bay substitution. This should be a key reason why the morphology of 4Cl-H2PDI aggregates is 0D NPs rather than 1D nanostructure. Fig. 2(A) reveals that with the increase of GQD amount in 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD samples, the π-π stacking peaks gradually move from 25.5° to 26.1°. It proves that GQD can act as 2D template and induce the closer stacking of 4Cl-H2PDI molecules, and enhance the π-π interaction of PDI moieties in 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD. Thus, the 2D nanostructure of 4Cl-H2PDI/4Cl-H2PDI can be found in Fig. 1(C). The interaction effect between GQD and 4Cl-H2PDI on their structures can be also identified through the change of Raman spectra of the C=C vibration peak at 1583 cm−1 and the C–H bending vibration peak in the perylene ring skeleton at 1293 cm−1 in Fig. 2(B) [40]. The increase in the I C
C/I C
H intensity ratio from 0.96 in 4Cl-H2PDI to 1.08 in 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD manifests their successful recombination and the interaction enhancement of PDI moiety.

The difference between 4Cl-PTCDA, H2PDI, and 4Cl-H2PDI in molecular structure can be clearly detected according to their FT-IR spectra in Fig. 2(C). For 4Cl-PTCDA, the peaks at 1764 and 1732 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of C=O, the peak at 1591 cm−1 is from the C=C vibration of the perylene ring, and the peaks at 653, 611, and 517 cm−1 belong to the C‒Cl vibration [41–43]. For H2PDI, the CN peak at 1334 cm−1 appears, while the C‒Cl peaks disappear. Meanwhile, the stretching vibration peaks of C=O bond blue-shifts to 1695 and 1654 cm−1 due to the formation of amide [44,45]. As compared with 4Cl-PTCDA and H2PDI, the C‒N, C‒Cl, C=C and C=O peaks obviously exist in the 4Cl-H2PDI sample. Fig. 2(D) reveals the change of 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD nanostructure with GQD doping amount. With the GQD increase, it can be found that two stretching vibration peaks of C=O at 1695 and 1651 cm−1 red-shift to 1698 and 1658 cm−1, and the C‒Cl vibration peaks at 653, 611, and 517 cm−1 blue-shift to 651, 610, and 510 cm−1. These phenomena mean that there are not only the π-π interaction, but also other forces between GQD and 4Cl-H2PDI. The C‒N vibration peak and C=C vibration peak are kept, indicating that the GQD addition should not distort the PDI framework in 4Cl-H2PDI molecule. In summary, the above characterizations confirm the successful synthesis of 4Cl-H2PDI and 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD composites, however, the interaction between 4Cl-H2PDI and GQD is still unclear.

The chemical state of different components in 4Cl-H2PDI and 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD composite were investigated to further study their formation mechanism of 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD composites with the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The survey spectra of 4Cl-H2PDI and 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD in Fig. 3(A) show the existence of C, N, O, and Cl elements, which is consistent with the element mappings in Fig. 1(F). By analyzing the proportion of sample elements, it is also found that the O element increases from 33.02 % to 34.04 %, and the C element also increases from 54.37 % to 55.23 % (see Fig. 3(A)) after the doping of GQD into 4Cl-H2PDI aggregates. For the C 1s XPS spectra of 4Cl-H2PDI in Fig. 3(B), the peaks at 286.25, 287.90, and 289.10 eV can be assigned to C–C/C=C/C–N, C–Cl, C=O, and O–C=O, respectively [32,46]. The new peak of 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD at 291.10 eV should derive from the characteristic peak of π-π* [43]. It can be clearly observed that after the formation of 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD composites, the C 1s binding energy, especially C–Cl specie, decreases by ∼0.1 eV. The Cl 2p XPS spectrum of 4Cl-H2PDI in Fig. 3(C) can be attributed to the electron transmission of Cl 2p1/2 (201.83 eV) and Cl 2p3/2 (200.15 eV) spinning orbit from the C–Cl covalent bond, and of Cl 2p1/2 (198.40 eV) and the Cl 2p3/2 (197.45 eV) from the chloride ion [42,43]. After the recombination of 4Cl-H2PDI with GQD, the whole binding energy of Cl 2p reduces and the percentage of Cl ion component obviously increases. The binding energy reduction of C 1s and Cl 2p proves the electron transfer from GQD to 4Cl-H2PDI in 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD under static contact. The increase of the Cl ion component strongly indicates the existence of the hydrogen bond (C‒Cl–H) between 4Cl-H2PDI and GQD. As a result, the C–Cl vibration becomes weak (see Fig. 2(D)). In Fig. 3(D), the O 1s XPS peaks of 4Cl-H2PDI at 531.10, 531.80, and 533.20 eV can be classified to C=O, O‒C=O, and adsorbed H2O, respectively. The position of corresponding peaks in the 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD composites only slightly shifts, however, the ratio of O‒C=O specie obviously decreases (see Tables S2 and S3). Thus, the H atom in Cl–H hydrogen bond should result from –COOH group in GQD. In addition, Fig. S9 shows that the C–N specie is stable before/after doping GQD, meaning that there is no strong interaction between GQD and the two ends of 4Cl-H2PDI. Overall, combining XRD, Raman, FT-IR, and XPS characterizations, we proved the formation of 4Cl-H2PDI molecule and 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD heterojunction, and the existence of hydrogen bond (C‒Cl–HOOC‒) besides the π-π interaction between 4Cl-H2PDI and GQD.

The influence of 4Cl-bay substitution of H2PDI and GQD-doping in the optical, photoelectric, and electrical properties was systematically investigated through ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectroscopy, photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy, transient photocurrent, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). In UV–vis spectra of H2PDI and 4Cl-H2PDI/DMSO solutions in Fig. 4(A) , four characteristic absorption peaks of perylene moiety, which originate from the coupling of four perylene vibration modes of 0-3, 0-2, 0-1, and 0-0 with the π→π* electronic transition, can be clearly observed [47,48]. Comparing the UV–vis spectra of H2PDI and 4Cl-H2PDI solutions, indeed, no new peaks can be seen after the 4Cl-bay substitution of H2PDI. However, the relative intensity of 0-1, 0-2, and 0-3 peaks at 490, 458, and 420 nm becomes stronger in 4Cl-H2PDI than H2PDI, indicating the effect of 4Cl-bay substitution on the structure and vibration of perylene moiety [47]. Meanwhile, the darker solution color of 4Cl-H2PDI than H2PDI also means that 4Cl-H2PDI can absorb more solar visible light (see Inset image). Fig. 4(B) shows the UV–vis spectra of different samples in the aqueous phase. For the H2PDI aqueous colloid system, two significant changes can be observed due to solvent effects and aggregation effects. (1) The intensity of 0-1 peak becomes stronger than that of 0-0 peak, meaning the formation of H-aggregate at ∼530 nm [35]. (2) The 0-0, 0-1, 0-2, and 0-3 absorption peaks of PDI moiety obviously blue-shift. For the 4Cl-H2PDI sample, the peak position in the aqueous is almost the same as that in the DMSO solution. Meanwhile, the 0-0 peak is stronger than the 0-1 peak, and the absorption peak of H-type aggregates of PDI can be not found. These mean that 4Cl-H2PDI NPs should be amorphous, as proved by their XRD pattern. With the amount increase of GQD in 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD composites, it can be observed that the absorption peak from GQD at 300–420 nm is enhanced. In addition, the electron transfer peak from 4Cl-H2PDI to GQD can be clearly observed at 530–700 nm, and become stronger with the GQD amount increased. The slight blue-shift of 0-0 peaks also reflects the formation of H-type aggregates of PDI, which agrees with the corresponding morphology (see Fig. 1). In addition, an obvious Tyndall effect of H2PDI and 4Cl-H2PDI aqueous colloid system can be observed, as shown in Fig. S10. In Fig. 4(C), the intensity decrease of PL spectra from H2PDI, 4Cl-H2PDI to 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD reflects that the photoinduced electron from the PDI group can be efficiently transferred to 4Cl substitutions and GQD rather than the de-excitation emission. Interestingly, Fig. 4(D) displays that with the increase of GQD doping in 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD, the PL intensity first decreases, and then increases. The PL intensity of 4Cl-H2PDI/0.2-mL GQD sample is the lowest. In Fig. S11, the corresponding excitation spectra prove that the emission peaks at 546 and 588 nm derive from the PDI group. Hence, the PL change also reveals the planarity decrease of H2PDI moiety with 4Cl-bay substitution and the planarity enhancement with GQD doping. These phenomena also reflect well the morphology change (see Fig. 1(A–C)).

To directly observe the ability of photoelectric conversion of different samples, their transient photocurrent values are tested using the solar light switch cycle, as displayed in Fig. 4(E). It can be found that as compared with GQD, 4Cl-H2PDI, and other 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD composites, 4Cl-H2PDI/0.2-mL GQD exhibit the highest photocurrent. The result further proves that the PL change of different samples should be closely associated with the charge transfer. The tip discharge effect and the gradual decrease of photocurrent may be attributed to two reasons. One may derive from the low mobility and quick recombination of photoinduced carriers due to their poor crystallinity of 4Cl-H2PDI (see Fig. 2(A)). Another may be associated with the consumption of photoinduced electrons owing to the part reduction of oxygen-containing groups in 4Cl-H2PDI and GQD. The EIS tests were carried out to investigate the surface resistance of different samples for the charge transfer (see Fig. 4(F)). Here, Rs is the solution resistance, Rct reflects the charge transfer resistance, and CPE1 represents a constant phase element at the electrolyte/electrode interface. Obviously, the impedance of the 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD composite is significantly reduced and the 4Cl-H2PDI/0.2-mL GQD has the lowest value. With the further addition of GQD, the impedance increase should be related to the appearance of isolated GQD in the 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD composite. Overall, the above characterizations enough confirm that the bay/end substitution of PDI and the GQD doping are beneficial to the light absorption, and the separation and transportation of photoinduced carriers. Especially, the 4Cl-H2PDI/0.2-mL GQD heterojunction with the most excellent photoelectric conversion and multiple active sites may have the best photocatalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic ORR activity for H2O2 evolution
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above research on the structure, component, and properties of 4Cl-H2PDI and 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD samples, furthermore, the effect of bay/end substitution of PDI and GQD doping on the photocatalytic ORR to H2O2 was carefully investigated, as displayed in Fig. 5 . In a typical test, 50.0 mg sample was dispersed into 100.0 mL of isopropanol aqueous solution (10 vol%) saturated with pure oxygen under a dark condition for 30 min. The 300 W Xe lamp was used as light source with a 420 nm cut-off filter. H2O2 was tested by iodimetry [34], as seen in Fig. S12. The detailed process can be seen in Supplementary Information. As shown in Fig. 5(A), no H2O2 can be detected after the raw 4Cl-PTCDA as a photocatalyst. After PTCDA was transformed to H2PDI with glycine (see Supplementary Information), the H2O2 evolution rate of 160 µM g−1 h−1 can be reached. The rate can be further increased by ∼7 times (1059.6 µM g−1 h−1) when H2PDI is replaced with 4Cl-H2PDI. These experimental results prove that the synergistic substitution of perylene-based material with bay and end groups can more efficiently promote photocatalytic H2O2 production. Fig. 5(B) displays that the as-synthesized GQD in our test has no activity for the photocatalytic H2O2 evolution owing to the absence of visible-light absorption (see Fig. 4(A)). However, its composites with 4Cl-H2PDI exhibit better activity than individual 4Cl-H2PDI, indicating that GQD should play an important role in the carrier transfer and/or interfacial reaction as a co-catalyst. With the 4Cl-H2PDI/0.2-mL GQD sample as a photocatalyst, the optimal H2O2 evolution rate of 2484 µM g−1 h−1 can be achieved, which is ∼16 times higher than that of H2PDI. Within 3 h, the rate (5482 µM g−1) also is ∼3 times that of individual 4Cl-H2PDI (1823 µM g−1), as seen in Fig. 5(B).

The effect of sacrificial agents and atmosphere on the photocatalytic H2O2 evolution rate of 4Cl-H2PDI and 4Cl-H2PDI/0.2-mL GQD was also carefully studied. Fig. 5(C) and Fig. S13 show that after the replacement of O2 with Ar or N2, no H2O2 can be identified, strongly implying that H2O2 should result from the ORR process rather than the direct oxidation of H2O (2H2O → H2O2 + 2H+ + 2e‒). When pure O2 is replaced with air, the H2O2 production rate decreases to 348.3 µM g−1 h−1, further confirming that the ORR process plays a crucial role. Without IPA as the sacrificial agent, the photocatalytic activity significantly degrades, and only the rate of 818 µM g−1 h−1 can be achieved in the presence of O2 and H2O. The activity is lower when the atmosphere is air. It means that IPA can effectively consume holes, thereby accelerating the separation of photoinduced electrons and holes. In Fig. S13, 4Cl-H2PDI as a photocatalyst shows a similar ORR activity to 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD. In addition, Fig. S14 also reveals that the optimal amount of 4Cl-H2PDI/0.2-mL GQD as photocatalyst is 50.0 mg in the tests. The thermodynamically spontaneous decomposition of H2O2 is also studied in the presence of different catalysts under light irradiation to further understand the H2O2 generation efficiency, as seen in Fig. 5(D). With the 0.1 mM H2O2 solution as an initial reactant, the decomposition order of H2O2 is H2PDI (55 %) > 4Cl-H2PDI (48 %) > 4Cl-H2PDI/0.05-mL GQD (30 %) > 4Cl-H2PDI/0.5-mL GQD (28 %) > GQD (24 %) > 4Cl-H2PDI/0.2-mL GQD (21 %) after 2 h. Obviously, GQD are more suitable to stabilize H2O2 than 4Cl-H2PDI during the ORR process. Hence, the 4Cl-H2PDI/0.2-mL GQD composite is more conducive to H2O2 production. In the cycling stability test (see Fig. 5(E)), the photocatalytic activity slightly decreases in the four tests. Especially, after the first test, the H2O2 evolution rate reduces from 2.3 to 1.8 µM g−1 h−1. For the following three tests, the activity is relatively stable. These phenomena can be attributed to two factors. One is from the GQD desorption from the 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD composite under high-speed centrifugation. Another should result from the unavoidable loss when the catalyst is separated from the photocatalytic system. After the test, the corresponding FT-IR spectrum, XRD pattern, and SEM of 4Cl-H2PDI/0.2-mL GQD were also measured. In Figs. S15–S17, no obvious change can be detected, confirming that the stability of 4Cl-H2PDI/0.2-mL GQD is very excellent. Here, the AQE of 4Cl-H2PDI/0.2-mL GQD was further studied under monochromatic light irradiation at 420, 475, 500, 520, 550, and 650 nm (see Fig. 5(F)). The maximal AQE is up to 0.75% at 520 nm. The tendency is consistent with its UV–vis absorption spectrum in Fig. 4(B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism analysis for photocatalytic ORR to H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: To gain insight into the photocatalytic mechanism of H2O2 production, the energy band structure of GQD and 4Cl-H2PDI were investigated through ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS), UV-DRS, VB-XPS, and Mott–Schottky (M–S) curve. Here, their UPS spectra are firstly measured and analyzed to investigate the electron transfer between 4Cl-H2PDI and GQD in the 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD heterojunctions, as shown in Figs. S18 and 6(A). The value of the work function (ɸ) of 4Cl-H2PDI and GQD is calculated to be 3.95 and 3.56 eV vs vacuum level, respectively. The details can be found in the Supplementary Information. The lower ɸ of GQD than 4Cl-H2PDI means the electron transfer from GQD to 4Cl-H2PDI in 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD heterojunction under the static contact until the Fermi energy levels of the two semiconductors reach equilibrium. As a result, the electron density on 4Cl-H2PDI increases, while it decreases on GQD, as accompanied by the bending of the energy band. Meanwhile, the built-in electric field (BIE) generates between 4Cl-H2PDI and GQD. Under light irradiation, the BIE will accelerate the rapid photogenerated electron transfer from 4Cl-H2PDI to GQD, thus promoting the ORR reaction to generate H2O2 on GQDs. As shown in Fig. S19, the band gap values of 2.02, 1.67, and 1.4 eV of H2PDI, 4Cl-H2PDI, and GQD can be obtained by transforming the absorption spectra based on Tauc’s method, respectively. Obviously, the twisted 4Cl-H2PDI structure is favourable to electron transmission and the separation of electrons and holes than H2PDI because of the narrower band gap [15]. In order to study the energy band structure and semiconductor type of these samples, their M–S curves were tested. In Fig. S20, the positive curve slope for H2PDI, 4Cl-H2PDI, and GQD samples reveals that they are n-type semiconductors [27]. The flat band potentials (E F) of H2PDI (−0.09 eV), 4Cl-H2PDI (−0.25 eV), and GQD (−0.79 eV) vs normal hydrogen electrode (vs. NHE) can be obtained according to the tangent intersection under different frequency test conditions. For the n-type semiconductor, the corresponding conduction band potentials (E CB) of H2PDI (−0.19 eV), 4Cl-H2PDI (−0.35 eV), and GQD (−0.89 eV) can be calculated on the basis of the equation, E CB = E F − 0.1 eV [14]. According to the formula of E VB = E g + E CB, the E VB values of H2PDI, 4Cl-H2PDI, and GQD are 1.83, 1.32, and 0.51 eV vs. NHE, respectively, which are consistent with the calculation results of UPS spectra and VB-XPS spectra (see Figs. S18 and S21). The detailed band structure is illustrated in Fig. 6(B) . Combined with the transfer direction of photogenerated electrons under light irradiation and the energy band structure in Fig. 6(A, B), it can be confirmed that the S-scheme heterojunction forms between the two n-type semiconductors in the 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD composites.

The photocatalytic H2O2 generation process is usually composed of two half-reactions, the ORR with E CB < 0.68 eV vs. NHE and the water oxidation reaction (WOR) with E VB > 1.76 eV vs. NHE. The related reactions can be seen in (Eqs. (2)–(7)). According to these equations, the energy band structure of H2PDI, 4Cl-H2PDI, and 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD conform to the ORR process, while only H2PDI meets the need of WOR. In order to further explore the ORR reaction pathway, the rotating disk electrode (RDE) tests of different samples are performed to obtain the LSV curves at different rotational speeds, and are converted into a Koutecky–Levich diagram (see Fig. 6(C) and Fig. S22). The electron transfer numbers of H2PDI, 4Cl-H2PDI, and 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD are 4.03, 2.17, and 2.26, respectively. With IPA as hole sacrifice agent (see Eq. (2)), for 4Cl-H2PDI and 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD, the H2O2 production process should mainly be the 2e‒ ORR (see Eqs. (3)–(5)), while H2PDI may involve 4e‒ transfer process of water oxidation to O2 (see Eqs. (5)–(7)). Generally, the ORR process can be divided into one step 2e‒ direct reduction (see Eq. (3)) and two-step single-electron reduction (see Eqs. (3) and (4)). In order to distinguish the two mechanisms, the superoxide radical (·O2 ‒) is detected through the electron paramagnetic resonance test (EPR) with DMPO as a capture agent under light irradiation, as shown in Fig. 6(D) [19]. Obviously, the ·O2 ‒ radical appears and increases with time for all samples. It means the presence of two-step single electron ORR process, which is kinetically more favorable than the one-step 2e‒ process. That is, O2 molecules are firstly reduced to the ·O2 ‒ radicals, and then further converted to H2O2. With p-BQ as ·O2 ‒ consumer and TBA as ·OH consumer, Fig. S23 displays that no H2O2 can be detected in the solution containing p-BQ, while H2O2 only slightly decreases in TBA solution. The two experiments further confirm that H2O2 should mainly come from the two-step single-electron ORR process with 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD and 4Cl-H2PDI as photocatalysts [6]. According to the energy band structure in Fig. 6(B) and Eq. (5), the ·O2 ‒ radicals in H2PDI should originate from the decomposition of H2O2 because of E VB > 1.44 eV vs. NHE. The concentration of ·O2 ‒ radicals with 3 min is 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD > 4Cl-H2PDI > H2PDI (see Fig. S24), revealing that lower E CB can promote the two-step single-electron ORR process, while high E CB of > 1.44 eV vs. NHE has disadvantage on the H2O2 stability. Here, the hydroxyl radical (·OH) was also detected with DMPO as a capture agent to evaluate the decomposition ability of the catalyst for H2O2 under light irradiation [19]. Fig. 6(E) and Fig. S25 reveals that the ·OH concentration is lower in 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD than 4Cl-H2PDI. The result reveals that the formation of S-scheme heterojunction should be helpful for the H2O2 stability, and the ORR should occur on GQD according to the decomposition test of H2O2 in Fig. 5(D). For H2PDI, the lower ·OH concentration is due to its lower activity for H2O2 production than 4Cl-H2PDI. In general, the S-scheme 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD heterojunction has more excellent photocatalytic activity for the kinetical ORR process than 4Cl-H2PDI and H2PDI. Here, it must be noticed that the pH value of the photocatalytic system is 6–7, thus, the reversible reactions in Eqs. (3)–(7) more easily occur in our experiments than theoretical ones.

In order to further understand the charge transfer and the synergistic photocatalysis between 4Cl-H2PDI and GQD under light irradiation, the femtosecond transient absorption (fsTA) spectra of H2PDI, 4Cl-H2PDI, and 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD nanomaterials in the aqueous phase were tested with the laser pump wavelength of 400 nm. For H2PDI in Fig. 7(A) , the negative ground state bleaching (GSB) peaks at 480 and 504 nm can be assigned to the S0→S1 electron transition of PDI group, while the negative peak at ∼557 nm is from the stimulated emission (SE) of S1→S0 and the absorption of PDI aggregate (PDI·PDI) * [32]. The positive peaks derive from the S1→S n /T1→T n excited state absorption (ESA) of the PDI group and aggregates. Consequently, the absorption peaks of the PDI+ cation at ∼493, ∼534, and ∼690 nm and the PDI− anion at 700–800 can be identified because of the formation of the polar excitons of (PDI·PDI)*, that is (PDI·PDI)* → PDI++PDI‒ [49,50]. In the fsTA spectra of 4Cl-H2PDI NPs (see Fig. 7(B)), the negative ground-state bleaching (GSB) peaks appear at 493 and 530 nm, accompanied by the ESA peak at 650–800 nm. Similar to the fsTA spectra of H2PDI, the corresponding ion peaks of the PDI group can be seen, too. The obvious changes are from the disappearance of GSB peaks from PDI aggregates and the red-shift of GSB peak from the PDI group. These changes are also consistent with the difference of H2PDI and 4Cl-H2PDI in the UV–vis absorption spectra. With the recombination of 4Cl-H2PDI with GQD, the fsTA peak position of 4Cl-H2PDI does not change. However, the four obvious phenomena can be observed in Fig. 7(C). (1) The intensity of corresponding ESA peaks of PDI‒ anion at 700–800 nm significantly decreases. (2) The negative GSB peaks become stronger relative to the ESA peaks. (3) The negative peak at ∼576 nm becomes stronger. (4) The ESA peak of PDI+ cation at ∼690 nm is stronger than those of PDI‒ cations at ∼730 and ∼776 nm. These differences enough confirm the photoinduced electron transfer from 4Cl-H2PDI to GQD under light irradiation and the formation of S-scheme heterojunction between 4Cl-H2PDI and GQD. In addition, the enhancement of PDI+ peak at ∼690 nm from H2PDI, 4Cl-H2PDI to 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD also reveals that the –COOH, –Cl and GQD can act as the electron-withdrawing groups under light irradiation.

Furthermore, the effect of 4Cl-bay substitution and GQD doping on the photoelectric conversion mechanism of PDI can be also understood through the separation and recombination lifetime of photogenerated carriers generated in PDI group. They can be obtained by kinetic fitting of corresponding GSB and ESA peaks (see Fig. 7(D)). Here, the lifetime of GSB peak from 0-0 mode light absorption of PDI can reflect the photoinduced carrier separation lifetime (τ CS), while the lifetime of ESA peak from the absorption of PDI‒ anion at ∼730 nm can represent the photoinduced carrier recombination lifetime (τ CR). In Fig. 7(D), it can be found that the τ CS and τ CR values of PDI chromophore in H2PDI, 4Cl-H2PDI, and 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD are 87.9 and 29.5 ps, 46.6 and 64.5 ps, 308.1 and 289.3 ps, respectively. As compared with H2PDI, a shorter τ CS and a longer τ CR of 4Cl-H2PDI mean that the 4Cl-bay substitution of H2PDI can promote the separation of photoinduced electrons and holes owing to the increase of dipole moment [51]. Furthermore, the electron transfer from 4Cl-H2PDI to GQD can greatly prolong τ CR, which also quickly quenches PDI excitons to the ground state. It reveals the reason why too much doping of GQD is not conducive to the photocatalytic process. Thus, both τ CS and τ CR of the PDI group in 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD become bigger. In addition, the kinetic fitting of the GSB peak of 0-1 mode and ESA peak of PDI‒ anion at ∼776 nm is also able to make the same conclusion, as shown in Fig. S26. In general, the 4Cl-bay substitution of H2PDI and GQD doping in 4Cl-H2PDI may efficiently promote the generation, separation, and transportation of photoinduced electrons and holes, prolonging the carrier lifetime, thereby improving the photocatalytic activity.

Combining the energy band structure of 4Cl-H2PDI and GQD with a series of data analysis from FT-IR, XPS, UPS, EPR, and fsTA spectra, the photocatalytic H2O2 evolution mechanism with 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD as a catalyst can be speculated, as illustrated in Fig. 8 . Under the light irradiation, the bay chloride as a strong electron-withdrawing group promotes the generation, separation, and transportation of photoinduced electrons from PDI group in 4Cl-H2PDI. Due to the existence of π-π interaction and the Cl–H hydrogen bond between 4Cl-H2PDI and GQD, the 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD heterojunction forms after doping GQD in 4Cl-H2PDI. Consequently, the built-in electric field induces the photogenerated electron transfer from the PDI group, chlorine to GQD (PDI → Cl → GQD) under light irradiation. Thus, a fast channel of photoinduced electron transfer can be built. IPA as a sacrificial agent accepts the photoinduced holes from the PDI group. Meanwhile, it is oxidized to acetone and provides the protons for ORR at GQD. The adsorbed O2 molecules at GQD sites react with the photoinduced electrons and protons. They are firstly reduced to superoxide radicals, and then to H2O2. As a result, the photocatalytic H2O2 evolution can be realized at the active site of GQD through a two-step single-electron process.

In summary, we have successfully synthesized H2PDI, 4Cl-H2PDI, and 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD to study the effect of bay/end substitution of PDI-based semiconductors and its S-scheme heterojunction on the photocatalytic ORR to H2O2. Their morphology, structures, and components were systematically characterized and analyzed. The experimental results manifested that 4Cl-bay substitution of H2PDI weakened the π-π stacking of the PDI group, and changed the nanostructure of PDI-based materials from 1D to 0D. GQD can act as a 2D template to induce the formation of 2D nanostructure due to the existence of Cl–H hydrogen bond between 4Cl-H2PDI and GQD. Meanwhile, the UPS and fsTA spectra confirmed that the photoinduced electrons should transfer from 4Cl-H2PDI to GQD under light irradiation. It also confirmed the formation of S-scheme heterojunction between 4Cl-H2PDI and GQD in the 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD composites. Consequently, the 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD composites exhibited better excellent optical, photoelectric, and electrical properties for the photocatalytic ORR to H2O2. As compared with H2PDI as photocatalysts for ORR to H2O2 generation (160 µM g−1 h−1), 4Cl-H2PDI (1059.6 µM g−1 h−1), and optimized 4Cl-H2PDI/0.2-mL GQD (2484 µM g−1 h−1) achieved the ∼7 and ∼16 times rate of H2O2 evolution under the visible-light irradiation (λ > 420 nm). The corresponding energy band structure, RDE tests, and EPR results revealed that the photocatalytic H2O2 evolution in 4Cl-H2PDI, 4Cl-H2PDI/GQD should originate from a two-step singe-electron ORR process, which was different with the 4e‒ process WOR process and/or one-step 2e‒ ORR process in H2PDI. This work showed a new insight into the PDI-based photocatalytic H2O2 synthesis.

Bo Zhang: Resources, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Kang Li: Formal analysis, Validation. Renfu Li: Methodology, Software. Shoufeng Wang: Data curation, Formal analysis. Longtian Kang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing.
